original_abstract: Cyber-physical systems (CPS) are critical to modern infrastructure, but are vulnerable to faults and anomalies that threaten their operational safety. In this work, we evaluate the use of open-source Large Language Models (LLMs), such as Mistral 7B, Llama3.1:8b-instruct-fp16, and others to detect anomalies in two distinct datasets: battery management and powertrain systems. Our methodology utilises retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) techniques, incorporating a novel two-step process where LLMs first infer operational rules from normal behavior before applying these rules for fault detection. During the experiments, we found that the original prompt design yielded strong results for the battery dataset but required modification for the powertrain dataset to improve performance. The adjusted prompt, which emphasises rule inference, significantly improved anomaly detection for the powertrain dataset. Experimental results show that models like Mistral 7B achieved F1-scores up to 0.99, while Llama3.1:8b-instruct-fp16 and Gemma 2 reached perfect F1-scores of 1.0 in complex scenarios. These findings demonstrate the impact of effective prompt design and rule inference in improving LLM-based fault detection for CPS, contributing to increased operational resilience.
humanized_abstract: Cyber-physical systems (CPS) such as drones, autonomous vehicles and power tools are key components of modern-day infrastructure. Therefore, it is important that they are fault tolerant against any type of fault or anomaly, such that the system as a whole is safe to operate. We investigate the capabilities of open-source large language models such as Mistral 7B, Llama3.1:8b-instruct-fp16 and others for detecting anomalies in the data associated with two CPS case studies namely: (1) a battery management system (BMS) and (2) a powertrain system. To carry out our investigation, we implement the retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) framework along with a novel two-step approach consisting of (i) a feature-extraction step during which the model predicts a set of operational rules (which characterize the normal operation of a CPS) for the training and testing sets of sensor readings for a given dataset, and (ii) a rule-validation step in which these extracted rules are used to mark anomalies (faults) within a given set of sensor readings for detection and classification purposes. The baseline experiment involving the original prompt is validated on both battery and powertrain case studies. Results show that even though the original prompt provided very effective results for the battery case study, adjustments were necessary for the powertrain dataset in order to maximize detection and classification outcomes. Furthermore, providing information about rule inference within the prompt allows for an improved fault detection and classification accuracy for the powertrain system. Upon further experimentation involving Mistral 7B and the adjusted prompt within the powertrain case study, an F1-score of 0.99 was recorded. Moreover, Llama3.1:8b-instruct-fp16 and Gemma 2 are demonstrated to have an F1-score of 1 for this specific case study, showing their potential in respect to operational CPS safety when the respective prompt is applied.
